Then Ali-abum and Puzur-Assur returned into the Heartland to convert the remainder of the silver and gold. They will come back in five days, and then I will send Puzur-Assur to you, so that he may inform you about the case. Here Hanunu and Sahaya are saying that: 'Although their efforts have obtained no asium in the Heartland itself, then asium may be found right in the vicinity. Send the silver and the gold (to us), so that we may buy the asium and send it to the investor. Assur-samsi, [who is the friend of Ha]nunu, will [ ... ] the Huramean (king), and [no] asium should be allowed to enter Luhuzattiya. God forbid, he must not give any asium to the Luhuzattiyan (king), so as not to anger the investor'. 544
The reason why Luhuzattiya should be avoided is made clear from a statement in another related letter:
The man (the investor) has promised the palace a lot of asium, so write a message that neither your letter(s) nor the asium should be sent via Luhuzattiya. 545
The combined evidence of CCT 2, 48 and ICK 1,1 implies that Puzur-Assur and Ali-abum had succeeded in obtaining a tiny amount of amiitum on their second run into Hattum, whereas the supplies Hanunu and Sahaya had thought would be available in the vicinity of Hattum proved inaccessible. ICK 1,1 shows that the amiitum bought by Puzur-Assur and Ali-abum was entrusted to Sahaya in Luhuzattiya. He brought it to Hurama and deposited it in the house of an affiliated trader. Later Sahaya left Hurama and went to Kanes; where he, according to the testimony, deceived his ailing investor into believing that he had paid 8 shekels of gold in the palace ofKussara on behalf of Ali-abum. Regardless of the veracity in Sahaya 's claim, the debt that Ali-abum had incurred in Kussara on behalf of the partnership should probably be linked with the 15 1/2 shekels of amiitum that he and Puzur-Assur had managed to procure. IfAli-abum's purchase and his debt in Kussara are related, then Nashefappears to be correct in his claim that Kussara was considered a part of Hattum. Given that Kanes itself was located close to the Kizihrmak River, which marked the fron- tier between Kanes and Hattum according to Dercksen, it is odd that the members of the partnership would travel east and north from Kanes via Hurama and Luhuzattiya in order to enter Hattum. If Hattum was a less precise geographical term that included also some of the region east of the K1Z1hrmak, then this would explain both the records that distinguish Hat- tum from Kanes, Wahsusana and Purushaddum, and the strong presence of the cities east of Kanes in the Hattum cluster. An example of the eastern network of routes linking Hahhum, Hurama and Luhuzattiya to Hattum is found in the letters kt 91/k 407 and kt 91/k 290 (cf. also section 4.9):
